data.title: Hyster-Yale: A Challenged Stock For Longer-Term And Value Investors
data.description:
Investment thesis

If you visited the Seeking Alpha summary page for Hyster-Yale, Inc. (NYSE:HY) recently, you will have seen a red banner warning that “HY is at risk of performing badly.”

In this article, we will take an in-depth look at the warning and HY’s fundamentals.

While it’s true the firm is not performing well, there are also positive signs, especially for medium- and long-term investors.

About Hyster-Yale

Along with its subsidiaries, it is a materials handling company that specializes in lift trucks, or, what used to be known as fork lifts. According to its 10-K or 2024, it is globally integrated and offers a full line of “high quality, application-tailored lift trucks and solutions”.

It also has its Bolzoni segment, which specializes in attachments, forks, and lift tables for lift trucks. The Nuvera segment focuses on the design, manufacture, and sale of alternative power sources for lift trucks. Recently, management announced a “business realignment” of Nuvera, involving the “the development, manufacturing and commercialization of lithium-ion battery modules chargers, battery management systems and energy management services.”

And, what a difference a year makes. When I analyzed Hyster-Yale's Q1-2024 results, the company was enjoying a run of higher revenue, net income, and earnings. As I wrote in that May 11, 2024 article, "Hyster-Yale is back in the growth game, and I have rated it a Buy."

In the wake of that earnings report, on May 7, investors had bid up the share price from $59.02 to $72.01. But, as the investors and I were to discover, again, is that what can go up can also go down.

At the close on June 18, 2025, shares traded at $38.52, and it had a market cap of $695.91 million.

Latest earnings

Hyster-Yale announced its Q1-2025 earnings on May 6, 2025 and the results were not good. Note all the negatives in the “% Change” columns:

In the earnings summary, management explained that the declines mainly were due to lower sales of lift trucks in the Americas (North, Central, and South), as well as in EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Africa). Lower sales occurred because of “reduced lift truck market demand and bookings levels in the second half of 2024.”

The JAPIC region—Asia (including China), and the Pacific—produced better results, with revenue rising from $3.4 to $3.6 million.

Bolzoni’s revenues also fell year-over-year, from $96.2 million last year to $80.3 million this year. Its adjusted operating profit shrank from $3.3 million in Q1-2024 to $0.6 million in Q1-2025. In this case, management attributed the revenue decline to the planned phase-out of legacy products with lower margins.

Dividends

Investors looking for a silver lining might turn to the dividend. With the share price falling by nearly half in the past year, the dividend was 3.66% with the price at $38.52, at the close on June 18.

That was enough to earn an A grade for yield, while it received a C+ for safety, D+ for its growth rate, and B- minus for consistency.

Although its growth rate is slow, the current conditions didn’t keep Hyster-Yale from raising the dividend by 2.9% in May. Indeed, it has raised the dividend every year for the past 12 years.

That suggests management wants it to become a dividend aristocrat or more, by raising the dividend for 25 consecutive years; getting that would help push its share price higher. Achieving this or a similar designation would mark it as a dependable company since it has had the strength to raise the dividend through many economic and industry cycles.

At 3.66%, it may also attract aggressive income investors who buy for the yield but also want an opportunity to capture some capital gains.

Assessing the red banner warning

After clicking on the warning banner at the top of the firm’s summary page, the following message appears:

“Hyster-Yale, Inc. has characteristics which have been historically associated with poor future stock performance. HY has declining growth and decelerating momentum when compared to other Industrials stocks, to the point that it gets a Sell rating from our Quant rating system. Stocks rated Sell or worse by our Quant rating system have massively underperformed the S&P 500...”

But Hyster-Yale has not been alone in these challenging times for the industry. In April 2025, IBISWorld reported, “Revenue growth has been stunted overall because of suspended trade and industrial production activity amid the COVID-19 pandemic. Many companies suspended their operations temporarily because of government restrictions. Still, restrictions have substantially eased, enabling activity within the industrial production sector to resume.”

Analyst Marley Brocker added, “Still, industry revenue has been shrinking at a CAGR of 0.6% through the end of 2024 to reach $12.9 billion, including an expected dip of 0.7% in 2024 alone.”

This 10-year chart of revenue, net income, basic EPS, and normalized EPS shows how Hyster-Yale’s fundamentals have declined for three consecutive quarters:

Before writing off the company, though, we should consider some less obvious facts about it.

The first criterion for the Sell rating is “declining growth” and the Seeking Alpha system noted, “The company has EBIT Growth (FWD) of -21.67% while the Industrials sector median is 7.76%.”

On that issue, the chart shows basic earnings rose for eight consecutive quarters before declining for three quarters. So, management at Hyster-Yale does know how to make money for shareholders when the market for lift trucks is rising or at least not declining. As they noted in the 10-K,

“Fluctuations in the rate of orders for lift trucks, attachments and fuel-cell technology reflect the capital investment decisions of the Company’s customers, which depend to a certain extent on the general level of economic activity in the various industries the lift truck customers serve. During economic downturns, customers tend to delay new lift truck and parts purchases. Consequently, the Company has experienced, and in the future may continue to experience, significant fluctuations in its revenues and net income.”

Further, while the cycle is weak or weakening, it will reverse at some time in the near future. When it does, investors may have an opportunity to pull in capital gains, as they did between Q3-2022 and Q2-2024.

From another perspective, this is a matter of opportunity costs. Buying Hyster-Yale now would position investors for potential capital gains, since the share price is depressed. Of course, it may go much lower before it begins to rise.

On the other hand, investors might buy a stock that is now generating higher earnings and prices, and enjoy capital gains now. That opens the possibility of owning another stock for now and then moving back to Hyster-Yale when its markets begin an upcycle.

The second Seeking Alpha criterion for the Sell rating is “decelerating momentum”, as shown in this five-year chart:

Momentum is at a low ebb right now, and reached that point because investors haven’t liked what they’ve seen in the past three quarterly reports.

But momentum takes in more than just past share prices, it also has a predictive side. Essentially, momentum theory posits that when prices are trending strongly in one direction, whether up or down, they will continue to move in that direction.

In Hyster-Yale’s case, “decelerating momentum” signals that we should expect more price declines in at least the near future. To go a step further, this too, like declining growth, indicates investors should shift their money elsewhere now and then check out Hyster-Yale when it has regained its positive momentum.

Finally, all these considerations depend on timing the market correctly, and as many of us have learned, that comes with its own challenges. The alternative is to buy what might be considered good long-term stocks and stick with them through up and down cycles. That’s especially true if we can pick up those long-term investments at value prices, which is where Hyster-Yale is currently situated.

Growth prospects

That momentum prediction of more price declines might well become true, judging by the company’s outlook for this year:

Note that the slide discusses “working capital efficiencies”, “inventory management”, “rationalization and energy management solutions”. These are all worthwhile measures that will lead to higher net income and earnings in the future, but this year they mean higher costs and lower net income.

While Hyster-Yale does not provide any hard numbers in its outlook, two Wall Street analysts see 2025’s normalized EPS falling by 75%:

2024’s normalized EPS was $8.55, so a drop to $2.25 is drastic. The one analyst who offers estimates beyond 2026 does not see earnings returning to the 2024 level in the next five years. Both analysts have posted Down revisions.

If the share price follows earnings, as it usually does, then we can expect at least a year of capital losses rather than gains.

Valuation

This 10-year chart tells the story of a stock that topped out at around $100 in early 2021, and since has fallen by more than half, to $38.52 at the close on June 18:

Since the price has fallen so far, it receives good grades for valuation:

With these grades, has it become a value stock? Let’s consider some of the characteristics of value stocks and Hyster-Yale’s status on them:

  • It is a mature and generally stable business.
  • It is experiencing what I would consider a temporary issue, declining demand from the industries it serves.
  • Low P/E ratio: Yes, it has a P/E GAAP of 7.02, which is certainly low.
  • Its price/sales and price/book ratios are also low. The former almost halved in the past year, from 0.31 to 0.17. It’s a similar story when we look at price/book, which has fallen from 3.05 a year ago to 1.36 now.
  • It now has a relatively high dividend yield, thanks to the falling share price.
  • Margin of safety: The Omni Margin of Safety Calculator finds a margin of just under 2.0%. Sources: current estimated sales for 2025 come from the Wall Street analysts’ estimates; the breakeven sales figure comes from 2024’s cost of sales plus G&A and other expenses:

On balance, I believe Hyster-Yale is now a value stock, although its margin of safety is small. Still, as noted, the Wall Street analysts don’t see its earnings rising much in the coming years.

And, what price should we expect a year from now? Given the Wall Street forecasts of lower revenue and lower EPS, it’s hard to envision a higher share price. On the other hand, the company is still posting earnings, although they are expected to be smaller.

With these positive and negative signals, I’m going to set a one-year target that is the same as the close on June 18: $38.52.

The Wall Street analysts have a one-year target of $56.00, an increase of 44.59%. That seems far too optimistic, especially in light of their revenue and earnings estimates, and their individual ratings of one Buy and one Hold. Add to that the two Down revisions (none Up) and that target seems untenable.

With the fundamentals weakening over three consecutive quarters, I can no longer rate Hyster-Yale a Buy. I consider this a quality company and expect it to rebound in the future, but until I see better fundamentals, I rate it a Hold.

Finally, the Quant system has given it a Sell rating.

Risk factors

Let’s start with the likelihood the lift truck market continues to drag, which will keep Hyster-Yale’s revenues and earnings at their current low levels. In turn, that would mean share prices continue to slump rather than recover.

Tariffs and trade policy may drive up the costs of its inputs and production, but non-American producers may not, giving foreign competitors an edge.

The Nuvera realignment is a work in progress and may not deliver the benefits that are expected. This realignment came out of a realization that its fuel cell initiatives could not be effectively commercialized.

Members of the firm’s founding family control up to 77% of its Class A and Class B common shares. They may make decisions that favor the family rather than independent shareholders.

I have made assumptions, as have Hyster-Yale and other analysts, based on the future economic and political climate being relatively similar to the immediate past. Therefore, our assumptions may turn out to be incorrect and our forecasts wrong.

Conclusion

Are you a short-term or medium/long-term investor? Are you a growth investor of a value investor? Depending on your strategy, your reactions to Hyster-Yale will vary.

Short-term and growth investors will find little to interest them. On the other hand, if you are a medium/long-term investor or a value investor, you may see an opportunity.

Despite its current challenges, Hyster-Yale is a well-established company that is entrenched in cyclical markets. As the cycle turns, investors will stop shedding and start buying its shares again. How long that will take is a critical question.

data.title: Winmark Corporation: Shares Just Don''t Make Sense At These Levels
data.description:
One stock that I struggle to really understand is Winmark Corporation (NASDAQ:WINA). If you're not familiar with the enterprise, it operates as a franchising firm that franchises out certain retail concepts. By far, its most popular brand is Plato’s Closet, which serves as a retail chain that buys used clothing and accessories and sells them to its customers. It primarily focuses on catering to teenagers and young adults. Other brands include Once Upon A Child, Play It Again Sports, Style Encore, and Music Go Round.

The one positive thing about the company that I can see is that its franchise model brings with it strong profit margins. Cash flows are significant relative to revenue. But outside of that, I see little reason to be optimistic. Sales are more or less stagnant and the stock is priced at incredibly high levels, both on an absolute basis and relative to other specialty retailers. At the end of the day, I cannot help but to keep the company rated a ‘sell’ even though the stock is up 16.1% since I last reaffirmed it as a ‘sell’ in February of this year.

Shares deserve to fall… by a lot

As you will see shortly, Winmark Corporation is trading at significant multiples compared to what you would expect from a retailer. You would expect a stock as expensive as this to at least be growing rapidly. But that has not been the case. Take the most recent data as an example, which would be for the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company was $21.9 million. That represented an increase of 9% compared to the $20.1 million the business reported one year earlier.

This growth was driven by a rise in the number of locations that the firm has in operation. As of the end of the most recent quarter, it boasted 1,363 stores. That was up from the 1,350 it ended last year with, and it compares favorably to the 1,327 that the company had in the first quarter of 2024. Though this helped the company from a revenue perspective, there were certain weak spots. As an example, merchandise sales contracted from $1.1 million to $0.9 million. This was because of reduced technology purchases from its franchisees. The good news is that other aspects of the company did grow. Royalties revenue, for instance, ticked up from $17.3 million to $17.8 million because of the growth in locations that it has in operation. And leasing income shot up from $0.8 million to $2.3 million. However, that was only because of the settlement of outstanding customer litigation that the company was able to recognize in the first quarter of 2025.

The growth in revenue that the company enjoyed brought with it higher earnings and cash flows. Net income grew from $8.8 million to $10 million. If you do the math, the company achieved a 45.7% net profit margin in the first quarter of 2025. That compared favorably to the 43.8% reported the previous year. Like I said, the franchise business model of the company makes it possible for these strong profit margins. And as it grows over time, you can expect those margins to improve. Cash flow metrics have also gotten better. Operating cash flow grew from $13.4 million, or 66.7% of sales, to $15.1 million, or 68.9%. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a jump from $9.6 million, or 47.8% of sales, to $10.8 million, or 49.3%. Lastly, EBITDA for the company expanded from $12.4 million to $13.8 million, taking its EBITDA margin from 61.7% to 63%.

It is worth noting that things have not always been on an upward trend. Driven by falling leasing income as the company winds down that part of the business, and also driven by weak merchandise sales because of lower technology sales to its customers, revenue actually contracted from $83.2 million in 2023 to $81.3 million last year. Even so, net profits barely budged, dipping only slightly from $40.2 million to $40 million. As the chart above illustrates, other profitability metrics for the company ended up falling as well, with the exception of adjusted operating cash flow. But with the exception of the operating cash flow margin for the company, profit and cash flow margins improved year over year.

It's the firm's business model and the continued growth in the number of locations that it has in operation that have investors optimistic. It also likely helps that the company has net debt of only $38 million on its books. For a firm with a market capitalization of $1.51 billion, that is an insignificant amount of leverage. I would argue, however, that even with these attributes, shares look overvalued. If we annualize the results that we have seen so far for the year, we would get net profits of $45.5 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $49.7 million, and EBITDA of $59.8 million.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. These are the kinds of multiples that you would expect to see from a firm that not only has strong profit margins, but that is also achieving attractive growth on its top and bottom lines. That we don't have that here. Furthermore, while true comparable firms don't really exist for this name, shares do look expensive compared to other specialty retailers. In the table below, I compared it to five such enterprises. On a price to earnings basis, three of the five companies I compared it to were cheaper than it is. But when it came to the other two trading multiples, Winmark Corporation was, by a pretty meaningful margin, the most expensive of the group.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Winmark Corporation 37.8 34.2 28.3
Upbound Group (UPBD) 12.0 7.3 1.6
Sally Beauty Holdings (SBH) 4.9 3.8 3.9
Petco Health and Wellness Co (WOOF) 26.5 4.0 8.5
National Vision Holdings (EYE) 50.6 12.4 21.2
Sportsman's Warehouse Holdings (SPWH) 45.6 17.6 14.5

Takeaway

At this time, I cannot in good faith upgrade Winmark Corporation. The company might very well continue to grow, though that growth will be modest. Cash flows will probably continue to impress as well. But the fact of the matter is that shares just look so expensive, both on an absolute basis and relative to other specialty retail plays. This combination of facts makes me quite bearish and justifies maintaining a ‘sell’ rating for now.

data.title: Arch Capital: Despite Attractive Valuation, Investors Should Wait For A Pullback
data.description:
As I’ve covered in a previous article, I see Arch Capital Group (NASDAQ:ACGL) as an interesting company in the insurance sector, due to its strong fundamentals and good business diversification between specialty insurance, reinsurance and the mortgage segment. However, its share price performance has been relatively weak over the past few months, as the company’s operating momentum was weaker than expected during the first quarter of 2025 due to higher catastrophe losses related to the California wildfires.

As shown in the previous figure, Arch’s shares have been flat since January, being slightly below the overall stock market performance during the same period.

Regarding its operating performance during Q1 2025, Arch’s net written premiums increased by 10.5% YoY to $4.5 billion. This is still a very good result despite being a more modest growth compared to previous quarters, as competition in the Property & Casualty (P&C) industry remains intense and the company has been more selective regarding risk. Indeed, Arch has reduced its exposure to treaties where returns no longer meet its hurdle rates, showing that a strict underwriting criterion remains a feature of its business model and is not changing the way it does business to chase higher growth.

This was especially true in its reinsurance segment, where the company reported net written premiums of nearly $3.5 billion over the past quarter, being up by just 2.2% YoY. While Arch continues to grow premiums across casualty, property catastrophe and other property lines, it has reported lower premiums related to speciality lines and structured deals, leading to relatively low net premiums written growth during Q1 2025.

On the other hand, its net written premiums in the insurance segment increased by 25.2% YoY to more than $1.9 billion in Q1 2025, mainly explained by the acquisition of the U.S. MidCorp and Entertainment insurance business from Allianz (OTCPK:ALIZY), acquired last August 2024. Excluding this effect, its net written premiums increased by low single-digit, showing that Arch’s top-line growth was modest at both reinsurance and insurance segments excluding acquisitions during the first quarter of 2025. Additionally, the mortgage segment is usually a low-growth business, and this profile didn’t change recently, as net premiums written declined by 4% YoY to $266 million in Q1 2025.

Regarding its profitability, Arch was significantly impacted by California wildfires at the beginning of the year, both at its insurance and reinsurance segment. Its catastrophe losses amounted to $547 million in the quarter, related mainly to this event. Due to higher catastrophe losses, its combined ratio increased to 90.1% in Q1 2025, compared to 78.8% in the first quarter of 2024, justified by a higher loss ratio while the expense ratio was stable year-on-year. In the reinsurance segment the combined ratio was 91.8% during the last quarter, while in the insurance segment the company reported a small loss (combined ratio of 100.1% vs. 94.1% in Q1 2024).

This event was the main reason why Arch reported much lower profitability during the last quarter, a trend that was widespread across the P&C industry, as California wildfires were responsible for about $38 billion of insured losses in Q1 2025. Overall insured losses in the first quarter amounted to $53 billion in Q1 2025 according to Aon, being the second-highest total record for the first quarter after Q1 2011, and much higher than $17 billion of losses on average in Q1.

Arch was no exception, and its underwriting income was significantly hit by catastrophe losses, declining by 43% YoY to $417 million in Q1 2025. Nevertheless, its operations remained profitable, showing that its strong underwriting capabilities and diversified insurance and reinsurance portfolios lead to a sustainable business over the long term. Regarding its investment income, it amounted to $378 million in Q1 2025, representing a decline of 6.7% from the previous quarter. This decline was mainly justified by a lower investment portfolio, as the company distributed a special dividend of $1.9 billion in December 2024.

Due to this combination of lower underwriting and investment income, Arch’s net income amounted to $564 million in Q1 2025, down by 48% YoY. Nevertheless, this represented an annualized return on equity (ROE) ratio of 11.5% in the first quarter, still being a very good level of profitability, especially considering the significant catastrophe losses related to California wildfires during this period. As the company remained profitable and did not return all earnings to shareholders, its book value increased by 3.8% compared to the end of 2024, to $55.15 per share at the end of last march.

As the company’s balance sheet remains quite solid given that its debt-to-capital ratio was only 14.7% at the end of last march, Arch continues to return capital to shareholders through share buybacks. During the first quarter, Arch repurchased $196 million of it own shares and also made additional $100 million of share repurchases during April, showing that Arch remains committed to return significant capital to shareholders. However, investors should be aware that Arch does not distribute regular dividends to shareholders, being a negative factor for its investment case compared to other insurance companies.

Going forward, there seems to be some weakness across the P&C industry due to strong competition that is leading to lower profitability across several insurance lines, as the P&C cycle seems to be turning from a ‘hard’ market over the past couple of years. Despite that, Arch is known for its conservative approach to risk management and is increasingly being more selective regarding risk taking, maintaining underwriting profitability as one of its main business goals.

Taking this setback into account, it’s not surprising that current consensus is expecting lower profits over the next few quarters despite some top-line resilience, as the company’s is being more selective in risk taking. Moreover, increased macroeconomic uncertainty and potentially higher inflationary pressures related to the U.S. trade war can have an adverse impact on Arch’s loss ratio in the near future, being another reason for the company to be conservative in its underwriting standards. On the other hand, in the mortgage segment, operating conditions are not expected to change much over the coming quarters and losses should remain low due to its high-quality portfolio, leading to expected stable underwriting results from this segment in the near future.

Regarding the integration of the acquired business from Allianz, the company says that it’s going well and this is expected to generate some synergies over the coming months, potentially leading to a lower expense ratio in the insurance segment and being a supported factor for earnings growth ahead.

However, its net income for the full year is naturally expected to be much lower than compared to 2024, as lower profitability in the first quarter of this year will have a significant impact in 2025 earnings. Indeed, its net income is expected to be around $3.12 billion this year, representing a decline of 27% YoY, and its ROE ratio should be around 13.7% (vs. 22.8% in 2024). While this is still a very good level of profitability and Arch’s earnings are expected to recover from an unusual weak Q1, investors should note that the hurricane season is just beginning and strong hurricanes may lead to higher losses than expected, something that could lead to lower earnings ahead.

Regarding its valuation, Arch is currently trading at about 1.6x book value, practically unchanged compared to when I previously analysed it. This valuation multiple is in-line with its historical average of the past five years, as recent book value growth was not enough to lead to a higher share price. Compared to some of its closest peers, such as The Hartford Financial Services Group (HIG) or W.R. Berkley (WRB) this valuation seems to be somewhat low, given that its peer group trade slightly above 2x book value.

Conclusion

Arch Capital Group is a strong company in the P&C industry, but its recent operating performance was greatly impacted by catastrophe losses related to California wildfires. While this is a one-off event, market conditions have become tougher in recent months and growth ahead should be much more moderate compared to the previous couple of years, plus the hurricane season is only starting and can potentially be a headwind for earnings growth over the coming quarters. While its current valuation seems attractive to long-term investors, I think it makes sense to wait for a lower share price ahead to enter into a position, as the hurricane season is a risk that investors shouldn’t overlook in the short term.

data.title: Gold Rejects Newer Highs Again Despite Headlines - Tides Turning For The Precious Metal? (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Gold has bounced throughout the past week, supported by war headlines in the Middle-East; however, flows seem to change as the precious metal has failed to hold and break its intermediate $3,450 highs. Gold is now trading below the key $3,400 level.

Quick reminder that US Markets are closed today for Juneteenth, which leads to some movements and flows being subdued. Positioning had already been quite heavy on the long side, with many investors trying to capture the negative market sentiment.

Many traders already long the bullion is not helping for new highs, as already positioned traders imply less available bullets for bulls to keep pushing up prices - one of the reasons why Client Sentiment tends to be a contrarian indicator.

However, markets in their current volatility tend to surprise participants and erratic moves are far from out of the picture. Keep an eye on the performance of other safe havens such as JPY and CHF leading on the forex currency board, and US Treasury Bonds up at a similar time as Stock Indices find lower levels.

Gold 4H and 1H Technical Analysis

Gold 4H Chart

The precious metal had tested the key $3,450 psychological level and consolidated close to the highs, although failure from bulls to push prices to test the higher main resistance zone of 3,470 to 3,500 led to some profit taking.

The more volatile 4H 20-period moving average has become resistance, currently at $3,384 - any impulsive move above would invalidate any bearish scenario.

In the meantime, price action is pointing towards the 4H MA 200 located at $3,320 right in the confluence with the 3,300 key pivot zone mentioned in earlier analysis.

Gold 1H Chart

Hourly charts are showing similar signs of rejection of last week's highs, with both the 1H MA 20 and 200 coming in as resistance - something to keep a close eye on as these indicators act as pivots.

Any break above making the moving averages turn into support, though the probabilities for this aren't currently as high. Another element to add to the picture is the RSI hanging mostly in the lower boundary and still far from oversold, giving space for movement.

One thing to keep in check is if any lack of downside correction is met with a rebound, which would happen if market anxiety catches up to the current price action.

Do not forget that any swift reversal in price action is always a possibility in trading - always keep an eye on your biases and new headlines hitting the markets.

Safe Trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Lumber Prices And WOOD
data.description:
In a January 14, 2025, Seeking Alpha article on lumber and the WOOD ETF, I concluded:

WOOD’s twenty-year monthly chart shows that technical support is at the September 2022 $63.78 low, with resistance at the May 2021 $98.98 high. At around $76.30 per share on January 14, 2025, risk-reward dynamics suggest a nearly 1:2 ratio, favoring the upside. Moreover, the trend of higher lows and higher highs since the September 2022 support level points to a bullish trend in early 2025. I favor long positions in WOOD with stops below $62 per share. The odds favor another move higher in the lumber futures arena, supporting higher prices for the WOOD ETF product in 2025. The Los Angeles wildfires and the need to rebuild the major U.S. city will increase wood demand over the coming months and years.

Nearby CME lumber futures were at the $582 per 1,000 board feet level on January 14, 2025, and have moved slightly higher. Meanwhile, the WOOD ETF was $76.29 on January 14 and has declined. However, WOOD held above the $62 per share level, reaching a low of $66.25 on April 9 as tariff fears gripped stocks, lumber, and markets across all asset classes. Since then, WOOD has recovered, making scale-down buying an optimal strategy.

Lumber futures are slightly higher in 2025

The CME’s continuous physical lumber futures chart shows that the nearby contract closed 2024 at $580.50 per 1,000 board feet.

The monthly chart indicates that lumber futures reached a high of $699 in March 2025. The price at $618 in mid-June 2025 was higher than the closing level at the end of 2024.

Lumber remains a highly illiquid futures market, therefore, proxies such as the WOOD ETF and other lumber-related equities and ETFs that correlate with lumber prices over time and serve as alternatives to the futures arena.

The WOOD ETF owns international lumber-related companies

The top holdings of the iShares Global Timber & Forestry ETF include:

At $72.19 per share, the WOOD ETF had $198.70 million in assets under management. WOOD trades an average of 3,635 shares daily and charges a management fee of 0.41%. WOOD’s $1.09 blended dividend translates to a 1.51% yield, which covers the expense ratio in a little over one quarter.

Tariffs have weighed on the WOOD ETF, which has underperformed lumber prices in 2025

The WOOD ETF closed 2024 at $76.34 per share. While lumber prices are higher than the 2024 closing level in mid-June 2025, the WOOD ETF was 5.44% lower at $72.19 per share.

That April 2, 2025, “Liberation Day” announcement in the United States caused markets across all asset classes to plunge.

The daily chart shows that the WOOD ETF product reached a $66.25 per share bottom before turning higher. The ETF held a critical technical support level on April 7, 2025.

The chart shows that WOOD’s April 7 low of $66.25 per share held above the critical technical support level of $63.78, which was established at the September 2022 low.

The bullish and bearish cases for lumber and WOOD

The bullish case for the WOOD ETF and lumber prices is:

  • The Fed’s favored inflation indicator, the personal expenditures price index, or PCE, increased only 0.1% in April, putting the annual inflation rate at 2.1%, close to the Fed’s 2% target.
  • Lower interest rates over the coming months could increase the demand for new home construction as mortgage rates decline.
  • Bullish price action in the stock market since the early April 2025 correction could push WOOD and other lumber-related equities higher.
  • While the monthly chart highlights a sideways trend in the WOOD ETF, lumber futures have made higher lows and higher highs over the past year.

The bearish case for the WOOD ETF and lumber prices is:

  • The U.S. debt, at nearly $37 trillion, could keep interest rates elevated.
  • Moody’s recently downgraded U.S. sovereign debt, which has put upward pressure on interest rates.
  • If interest rates remain higher for longer, it will be challenging for lumber and the WOOD ETF to rally. The Fed kept the short-term Fed Funds Rate unchanged at the June FOMC meeting.

The bottom line is that bullish and bearish factors are pulling lumber prices (and the WOOD ETF) in opposite directions in June 2025.

Raise WOOD stop levels to below $65 per share

Buying the WOOD ETF on price weakness since mid-January 2025 has been optimal, as the ETF dropped from $76.29 to $66.25 and recovered to over $74 per share. I suggested a stop on long positions below the $62 per share level in my mid-January article. In June 2025, the stop should now be below the $65 level, as the April 7 low of $66.25 is now the critical technical support level.

WOOD’s Seeking Alpha ETF grades reflect the recent price action with an F in momentum. The 0.41% management fee receives a B- in expenses, while the blended dividend earns a C grade. Volatile lumber prices validate the D+ in risk, while WOOD’s assets under management and daily volume earns a C- in liquidity.

Lumber futures are highly illiquid, which makes trading or investing in the futures arena dangerous. Meanwhile, the WOOD ETF is closely correlated with lumber prices and serves as an acceptable proxy for market participants seeking long exposure to lumber prices. The underperformance compared to lumber prices since the end of 2024 makes the WOOD ETF a buy at the current price level.

data.title: Ciena: Reasonable Valuation And Attractive Growth Outlook (Upgrade)
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on Ciena Corporation (NYSE:CIEN) in Dec’24, which I downgraded to a hold rating as the valuation was no longer attractive, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. I am now upgrading the stock. The 2Q25 results confirmed that CIEN is entering a powerful new growth cycle. AI demand is no longer just a narrative; it’s showing up in the numbers. Cloud orders are doubling, new AI-specific use cases are emerging, and service provider demand is rebounding.

2Q25 Earnings Review

Total revenue grew ~24% y/y to $1.13 billion, at the high end of guidance. By segment, networking platforms grew 28.1% y/y to $866.3 million, driven by a 38.1% y/y growth in optical networking. The software segment also did very well, growing ~14% y/y with Blue Planet revenue up 93.6% y/y to $28 million. On the margin side, adj. gross margin fell 250 bps y/y to 41%, but this was mainly driven by a higher mix of new solutions, which carry lower initial margins. Higher incentive compensation linked to strong bookings also pushed up OPEX. While this margin pressure may raise concerns, I see it as front-loaded investments due to the strong demand. If anything, this validates the narrative that CIEN is benefiting from the optical upgrade cycle.

Cloud and AI Demand

2Q25 results validated that AI-driven network demand is a major catalyst for CIEN. The surge in demand must be very strong, so much so that management described the market as experiencing a “step function” in demand, which I infer as a sharp inflection and not just a mere recovery. We can see this playing out very clearly in the cloud provider segment, which saw 85% y/y growth in 2Q25, growing to a record $428 million (now accounts for 38% of total revenue). Remember, this segment grew 0% in 1Q25 and was down between 2-4Q24.

The impressive part here is the order momentum: cloud provider orders are on track to double in FY25 (per management). Notably, three of the company’s top five customers in 2Q25 were hyperscalers, which goes to show that CIEN is being seen as one of the go-to vendors in supporting massive AI workloads. More broadly, I think these figures have a very positive impact on the stock’s narrative, as AI-driven growth is no longer just a narrative in the air but is a real growth driver with tangible numbers to back it up.

Strategic Wins Confirm Expansion into New High-Value AI Applications

CIEN is not just gaining a lot of momentum from the secular tailwind; it is also further embedding itself into more parts of the value chain, and this adds more growth drivers to the equity story. CIEN announced two major design wins that demonstrate this movement, where it is moving beyond traditional data center interconnect [DCI].

The first is the deployment with a global cloud provider to connect GPU clusters that are distributed in different regions. Remember that I called this out previously, that hyperscalers are being forced to spread their AI training workloads across multiple sites, creating demand for extremely high-capacity optical connections over long distances. This is playing out as expected, and revenue from this win will begin flowing in late FY25 and is expected to scale into FY26. The broader implication is that CIEN has proven it is able to capture a larger share of the entire AI-infrastructure market.

The second win is an out-of-band management network inside the data center. This is less impactful than the first, but the idea is the same. This shows that CIEN expertise in optics can be used even for intra-facility use cases.

If we piece these developments together, what we have is that CIEN's total addressable market has expanded, and I believe this has significantly expanded the growth runway for the business.

Service Provider Recovery Solidifies as MOFN Activity Hits All-Time Record

While the momentum in the cloud is the main highlight for CIEN, the recovery in the service provider market is also becoming a powerful growth engine. In my last write-up, I noted that there were early signs of demand stabilization from this customer base. The 2Q25 results confirm that this trend is not only real but accelerating. Management described a clear “improving trend line,” particularly among Tier 1 North American carriers, where inventory normalization is now largely complete.

What makes this entire dynamic compelling and very positive is that it gives CIEN another avenue to tap into the booming AI-driven infrastructure buildouts. And what validated this is that CIEN has achieved an all-time record for MOFN (Managed Optical Fiber Network) activity in 1H25. This suggests that service providers are actively building out dedicated network capacity for cloud partners, which means CIEN is indirectly benefitting from AI-driven infrastructure buildouts even when not selling directly to hyperscalers.

Yeah, we're seeing a lot of MOFN activity in India. It's not translated to a massive amount of revenues yet, but we've taken the orders and it will. We're seeing MOFN activity throughout the globe. And I think it's just really -- it goes hand-in-hand with the whole cloud buildout. They can't build everywhere with their own network, and so it's augmented by these MOFN opportunities. We've seen that in North America as well. There's been some, as you know, large announcements around some of the larger carriers partnering there, with Lumen and Zayo, et cetera. We're seeing that reflected in pretty much all parts of the globe. Asia, outside of India, we're beginning to see that on -- in Europe. We've seen activity, it's the largest amount of activity that we've seen, and it really just goes hand-in-hand with the overall strategic plans for these cloud providers. 2Q25 call

Valuation

Thankfully, CIEN’s valuation has come down to a more palatable level. The stock used to trade at 30x forward PE (which I thought was too expensive), but at the current 25x, I see a plausible path towards 21% upside even if I assume multiples continue to contract. What changed in my current model is that I increased the revenue growth outlook to reflect the order momentum and recovery in the service providers market. But I kept my margin assumptions the same. This translates to a net income expectation of $632 million. Attaching 20x to it, which is the high end of CIEN’s historical trading range, I got a share price target of $89. The interesting thing here is multiples may not contract at all, since CIEN is now entering a strong growth cycle with numbers to back it up. Suppose the market continues to value CIEN at 25x; the upside is a lot more attractive than what I modeled.

Risk

While demand is strong, near-term margin could stay depressed for longer than expected if it continues to capture new deals. This may look optically negative and cause pressure on the stock. CIEN also has meaningful customer concentration, particularly among cloud and Tier 1 service providers. Any slowdown in spending from these groups would have a direct impact. Lastly, CIEN growth may face periods of lumpiness as AI infrastructure buildout demand can be lumpy, and this may cause CIEN to report periods of slow growth that may spook the market.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my recommendation for CIEN is an upgrade to buy. I like the demand visibility, improved positioning in this AI infrastructure space, and also tangible evidence of recovery in the service provider market. With valuation now at a cheaper level, I see a path towards >20% upside.

data.title: KBWR: High LTD Ratios, Weak Fundamentals Are Reasons To Avoid Small Regional Banks
data.description:
Investment Thesis

There aren't many compelling reasons to buy the Invesco KBW Regional Banking ETF (NASDAQ:KBWR). In this article, I'll discuss a concerning trend in loan-to-deposit ratios for small commercial banks and compare KBWR's fundamentals to those of four other banking ETFs, which reveals its flaws in terms of quality and growth factors. As a result, I don't see a good investment opportunity at the moment, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

KBWR Overview

Strategy Discussion

KBWR tracks the KBW Nasdaq Regional Banking Index, which, according to its methodology document, "is designed to track the performance of U.S. regional banks and thrifts that are publicly traded in the U.S." I've summarized the main points from that document below.

1. Eligible securities must be classified as engaged in regional banking activities as determined by the Index Committee and meet specific security type, liquidity, and float requirements.

2. At each rebalancing period, the Index employs a modified market capitalization weighting methodology, such that no securities exceed a 4% allocation and that no more than five securities reach that cap. After the Index applies this first weight cap, all remaining securities (i.e., those outside the top five) are capped at 2%.

3. The Index rebalances after the third Friday each March, June, September, and December.

Since the Index Committee has full discretion when applying the "regional bank" designation, this has enormous implications for what KBWR holds. For example, I found it excluded 90 stocks in the SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE), whose holdings are guided by Global Industry Classification Standards, or GICS. Many of those exclusions are larger stocks, so while the Index follows a modified market cap weighting scheme, the holdings are mostly in the small-cap category.

Peer Group and Performance

In this article, I will compare KBWR with four ETFs, as follows:

  1. Invesco KBW Bank ETF (KBWB)
  2. SPDR S&P Bank ETF (KBE)
  3. iShares U.S. Regional Banks ETF (IAT)
  4. SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE)

All ETFs have a 0.35% expense ratio except for IAT, which charges 0.40%. They're also all well established, with about a 14+ year track record, though KBWR's $46.50 million in assets under management is tiny by comparison.

Since its inception on November 1, 2011, KBWR has delivered a 233.89% return, in line with the two other regional bank ETFs, IAT and KRE. Meanwhile, KBWB and KBE, which also include diversified banks, outperformed with gains of 358.94% and 277.40%, respectively.

Comparing KBWR and KBWB is a good way to distinguish the returns between small regional banks and large U.S. national money centers. By design, the two do not overlap, so with KBWB, you will gain exposure to large banks and brokerages like JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Goldman Sachs (GS), and Morgan Stanley (MS).

As shown below, these banks have regularly beaten their smaller counterparts on the rolling five-year returns chart. For the month ending May 31, 2025, KBWB was up by 2.37% per year (14.47% vs. 12.10%), and the gap almost reached 6% per year in October 2021.

KBWR Analysis

LTD Ratio Trends: Large vs. Small Banks

Over the last 15 years, the gap separating the average annual loan-to-deposit ratio for small and large domestically chartered U.S. commercial banks has steadily risen. As shown below, loans as a percentage of deposits were actually smaller for small banks by about 6%, but now, the average LTD ratio for small banks is 19% higher for the first five months of 2025.

As noted in this article published by the Philadelphia Federal Reserve from Q3 2017, "deposits...are a stable source of funding for banks...a bank that finds itself with too few deposits to fund loans must rely more heavily on non-deposit sources of funds, whose availability and price are much more sensitive to changing economic or financial conditions." The authors expressed concern that a rising LTD ratio for small banks could indicate comparatively higher liquidity risk and lower lending standards, and based on my extension of the analysis to 2025, the LTD ratio for small banks has consistently remained above 80%, except for a brief reprieve to the low 70s due to the COVID-19 pandemic. I suggest these persistently high LTD ratios are why investors have steered clear of the small banks held by KBWR, and why it's lagged KBWB substantially since its inception in 2011.

KBWR Fundamentals By Top 25 Holdings

KBWR isn't a leader on any key fundamental metric, either. As shown in the table below, which covers the fund's top 25 holdings (60.50% of the total), KBWR's profitability metrics are below average, its valuation ratios are only average, and its historical and forward-looking earnings per share growth are generally poor, especially compared to KBWB.

Here are three additional observations to consider:

KBWR: It Leans Small

Just to cover my earlier point about how KBWR has a small-cap lean, consider the difference between its weighted average market cap and KBWB's ($4.94 vs. $169.08 billion). Its market cap is also smaller than IAT and KRE, and as shown below, 74.95% of its holdings are assigned to the small-cap segment, based on the latest breakpoints used to divide the S&P 1500 Composite Index (less than $7.4 billion). That's 39.43% above the peer group average.

KBWR: Quality Metrics At/Below Average

KBWR's weighted average ROA is only 0.96%, which is the lowest of the five. The same goes for its 8.66% ROE figure, and except against KBWB, its 26.85% is also behind its peers. These metrics all feed into the fund's 5.01/10 profit score, which I derived from each security's Seeking Alpha Factor Grade. To be sure, neither fund's score is great, but IAT appears to be the highest-quality fund of the five at the moment.

KBWR: Growth Rates Lacking, Valuation Only Average

The fundamental analysis table shows KBWR's constituents have increased earnings per share by 2.53% per year over the last year, which includes 3.38% over the last year. In contrast, KBWB's 26.30% growth rate last year represented a massive turnaround, and it's no surprise that it outperformed KBWR by 23.96% last year and is up another 10.66% YTD through June 18.

Earnings growth is what's driving bank stock returns, and based on consensus one-year earnings per share estimates, the trend should continue in favor of KBWB (14.47% vs. 8.66%) and, to a lesser extent, KRE (10.09%). In my view, there's not a compelling valuation reason to pick KBWR - its 10.84x forward P/E ratio is close to average, and even though its 1.03x trailing P/B ratio is attractive, it's not enough to offset its growth deficit.

Investment Recommendation

After reviewing KBWR's performance, fundamentals, and LTD ratio trends for larger and small U.S. commercial banks, I don't believe it's a compelling buy. I'm concerned that LTD ratios are back above 80% for many of the small banks held by KBWR, and from an earnings growth perspective, its holdings also aren't bouncing back as strongly as their larger counterparts. I might pick KBWR over the more diversified KRE, but for now, my suggestion is to stick with the larger, liquid, and more profitable large banks. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to the discussion below.

data.title: Robinhood: All Good Things End With Valuation (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Robinhood (NASDAQ:HOOD) has proven to be tariff-resistant as it continues to benefit from incredible growth in transaction revenues. The company has made a solid push into GAAP profitability and management has committed to a share repurchase program. The company maintains a net cash balance sheet and the Gold membership is resonating with consumers. I fear that the current valuation has gotten ahead of itself, as it is pricing in many years of growth. I am downgrading the stock to a neutral rating until the fundamentals catch up with the stock price.

HOOD Stock Price

I last covered HOOD in February, where I upgraded the stock to a buy rating on account of my view that the company would take market share long term. The stock is up 23% since then.

I am backtracking my positive coverage as I fear that I may have incorrectly lowered my return hurdles amidst an elongated bullish run.

HOOD Stock Key Metrics

HOOD is a brokerage firm which is known for its popularity among more-speculative retail investors. That reputation might be undeserved given the company’s aggressive product velocity - I prefer to instead view it as a rare innovator in a historically innovation-barren brokerage industry. Many of its “innovations” tend to be easily replicable and eventually adopted by competitors, but being first can mean market share gains.

The latest quarter saw the company continue its rapid growth, with total platform assets jumping 70% YoY to $221 billion. I note that roughly $40 billion of assets were brought over from its acquisition of TradePMR.

The company’s Robinhood Gold product has continued to gain in popularity, as evidenced by the adoption rate rising to 12.4% in the quarter.

The company saw net revenues jump 50% YoY, powered by 77% YoY growth in transaction-based revenues.

That 77% growth rate came in large part due to a 100% jump in crypto transaction-based revenues, though equity and options transaction revenues grew rapidly as well. I note that the company is beginning to lap tough comparables, and transaction-based revenue growth rates should moderate within the next 2 to 3 quarters.

The company saw its adjusted EBITDA jump 90% YoY to $470 million, representing a 51% margin.

The company was GAAP profitable for the 6th consecutive quarter, though I caution that there appears to be some cyclicality in this business, with investors appearing to trade more frequently during bullish rallies.

HOOD ended the quarter with $4.6 billion in cash and investments versus no debt.

The company repurchased $322 million of stock in the quarter, marking a meaningful uplift from prior quarters but reasonable given the booming profits. It appears that one driver of the accelerated share repurchases was to offset the dilution from the TradePMR acquisition.

On the conference call, management noted that they had increased the authorization to a total of $1.5 billion with expectations to deploy $800 million over the next two years with “flexibility to accelerate if market conditions warrant.” Management also noted ambitions to eventually allow access to private markets, which would mark one of the bigger innovations in recent years.

Is HOOD Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, the stock traded at around 19x sales. Consensus estimates call for a steep deceleration in top-line growth moving forward.

It is hard to say if these consensus estimates are too conservative or too optimistic. On the one hand, HOOD looks capable of taking market share for many years to come. On the other hand, the vast majority of its growth appears to be driven by a surge in transaction revenues, something that seems to be highly cyclical in nature. The company will also be lapping tough comparables on that increased engagement moving forward.

Even if we assume that the company can sustain around 16% growth for the next 5 years, the stock still looks richly valued. I assume that HOOD can sustain 34% net margins over the long term, which would put it in the same league as Charles Schwab (SCHW). This should be considered an aggressive assumption, given the heavy reliance on promotions and marketing in driving the growth rates here. That places the stock at around 55x long-term earnings, which would represent a 3.4x price to earnings growth ratio (‘PEG ratio’) based on 16% projected growth. While such a high PEG ratio is not unheard of and does not necessarily scream “bubble,” I typically look for stocks in the 1x to 2x range as the lower valuation would allow for a greater chance of outperformance. I would be more interested in the stock if it traded under $45 per share, which would represent an 11x sales multiple and would still be at the higher end of the fair value range.

HOOD Stock Conclusion

HOOD has performed very well, but all good things end with valuation, as it is perhaps not so commonly said in the investing world. It might seem curious to be downgrading the stock even with strong revenue growth, torrid margin expansion, a net cash balance sheet, and ongoing share repurchase program, but the valuation looks quite stretched to me. It is anyone’s guess when the bullish rally in the broader market or HOOD come to an eventual end, but buying stocks at such aggressive valuations sets the stakes too high, especially as there remains many other attractive opportunities among high-quality growth stocks like those I have found for the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am downgrading HOOD to a neutral rating.

data.title: Utz Brands: A Snack Stock To Watch, But Not To Bite Just Yet
data.description:
Thesis: Reasonably Valued, But Not Yet a Buy

Utz Brands, Inc (NYSE:UTZ) isn’t salty enough to chase just yet, but it’s a name worth keeping on your watchlist. Utz is trading at a 15.5x forward P/E multiple, slightly below the peer average of 16.5x. I believe Utz is fairly priced given its minimal revenue growth and modest forward EPS CAGR at 11% for the period 2025-2027. The stock is not yet attractive to buy, with a low dividend yield of 1.84% that lies below both the peer group and the sector median. Utz’s management has a plan under execution to increase its market penetration across the U.S., as reflected in the 1.6% revenue growth QoQ. In contrast, Utz has yet to improve its net margin, which stands below the sector median. Altogether, I’m rating UTZ a Hold, until we see improvements in the next quarters in both the top and bottom lines.

Utz Brands: 100 Years of Flavor, Focused on the Power Four

Utz Brands is an established American snack food company, founded in 1921, based in Hanover, Pennsylvania. Utz operates and sells its products in the U.S. Utz manufactures a wide portfolio of snack products, primarily focusing on potato chips, pretzels, cheese snacks, and pork rinds.

Utz owns a number of flagship brands in the snack food category, with a focus on what it calls the “Power Four Brands”, which are Utz, Zapp’s, On the Border Chips & Dips, and Golden Flake. These brands are considered the core growth engine for the company.

Utz currently operates in 20 states, which represent its core market, and accounts for 56% of its net sales, with a 6.5% market share of retail sales, as noted in the Q1 presentation. Utz’s management plans to expand in the other 30 states, in which it holds a 3% market share, as of Q1. It’s notable that its retail sales increased at a CAGR of 7.2%, which is impressive, given its competition with major players in the salted snacks industry, like Frito-Lay, which dominates the majority of the market.

Utz Grows Volume, Not Margins: A Clean-Label Play With Long-Term Potential

Currently, Utz is expanding its portfolio through innovative products, especially in its better-for-you snacks segment. In 2025, Utz added new products to the Boulder Canyon and On The Border brands, like tortilla chips and ridged potato chips cooked in avocado oil and GMO-free.

I think focusing on niche segments like clean labels through its brands Boulder Canyon and On The Border, would be more effective for driving Utz’s growth rather than relying on basic salted snacks, which is a mature highly competitive market.

On a quarterly basis, revenue grew by 1.6%, driven by 4.9% organic net sales growth in branded salty snacks. This growth was mainly led by Boulder Canyon, as noted in the presentation. The surge in Boulder Canyon sales implies that Utz is gaining traction in the clean-label snack segment.

Revenue growth was slightly offset by a 3.4% decline in net price realization. The reason behind this is that Utz made offers and discounts to increase first-time purchases, especially in the expansion states, according to the earnings call.

Utz’s branded snacks segment contributed 87% of total sales, while non-branded snacks accounted for 13%. This represents a decline from Q1 2024 when non-branded snacks contributed 18% to revenue. The dip is attributed to a decline in partner brands and private label business, in addition to the disposition of assets, according to management.

I speculate that this move reflects Utz’s pivot toward focusing on brand strength in order to gain more market share and enhance its pricing power.

Given that the private label space not only carries lower margins than branded products but also exposes Utz to competition with larger private label players, who could offer better prices than Utz.

As shown in the table below, gross profit declined modestly by 1.17%. The decline was attributed to increased promotional spending and investment in capacity and distribution, according to the 10-Q. It may seem unusual to see gross profit margin decline, despite volume growth. However, Utz’s decision to offer packs with more volume at the same price was necessary to gain more market share in expansion states, in addition to investing in its capacity. Given the fact that it still holds just an average of 4.75% market share nationwide, which implies significant room for growth.

GAAP net profit grew in dollars by 137%. However, This figure can be misleading because Utz faced a distorted Q1 2024. Its net profit was disrupted by the sale of assets and higher income taxes. Additionally, a non-recurring loss warrants liability. Hence, it will be more accurate to compare its profitability to the sector median rather than its historical performance. Utz’s trailing twelve-month net income margin at around 2% is well below the sector median of roughly 4%. However, I think expanding in clean label space and more concentration on branded products could improve its profitability margins upward in the long run.

To sum up, Utz showed mixed signals, evidenced by revenue growth but compressed margin. However, I don’t see the margins' compression as a red flag, it’s more likely a sacrifice by management of short-term profitability for long-term growth, considering that Utz has significant room to grow its market share.

GAAP Net Profit Margin

1.6%

0.6%

1%

Branded Focus and Cost Discipline Power Utz’s Positive EPS Trajectory

The consensus revenue estimate for the full year is around $1.42B, which represents a year-over-year growth of 1.5%. It’s worth noting that Utz’s revenue in FY 2024 declined YoY because of the divestiture of its Good Health and R.W. Garcia brands, which lowered its sales volume. However, this move also shows Utz’s strategy to pivot toward high-growth segments and focus on its core brands.

According to SA analysts’ estimates, earnings per share are expected to continue on their growth trajectory. Management also guided toward 10%-15% adjusted EPS growth.

The growing EPS is most likely to be attributed to continued sales momentum driven by Power Four Brands, especially Boulder Canyon's continuously expanding SKUs.

Furthermore, Utz aims to deliver $150MM in productivity savings through 2026.

Management initiated the saving program in 2024, and this was reflected in Q1 results, evidenced by a 100 basis point improvement in adjusted gross margin, which helped partially offset high promotional spending. The program could help offset the impact of management’s plan to invest in distribution and increase its capacity, as stated in the earnings call. Altogether, the future outlook looks good with a CAGR EPS growth of 11.7% for the period 2025-2027, as shown in the chart below. Market share growth also will support its growth trajectory.

Relative Valuation Analysis: Room for Growth or Fairly Priced?

As presented in the table below, UTZ appears to be priced slightly below the peer average, implying a possible undervaluation. From a forward P/E perspective, the market is pricing Utz below JJSF and VITL. This could be attributed to a healthier net margin of 4.79% and a broader product mix for JJSF.

I had the chance to cover VITL previously, so I don’t think UTZ is going to be rated anywhere close to VITL’s P/E ratio, considering that VITL has a higher growth potential and an outstanding net margin of 8.26%.

At the low end of the peer group are KLG, Dole, and NOMD. To start off with KLG, its EPS forward growth is nothing to compare with Utz. Given that Utz still has significant room for growth, unlike KLG which is already well-penetrated in the U.S. cereal market.

DOLE is trading at a cheaper price-to-earnings compared to Utz, due to DOLE’s weak profitability margins, with a gross margin of 8%, well below the sector median. Its net margin of 1.1% is also weaker than Utz’s 2%.

NOMD, a stock that I have also covered before, is trading at a discount because of its high exposure in the European market and the level of maturity it has achieved, which hinders its growth.

It’s understandable to see Utz offering a low dividend yield compared to its peers, given that it reinvests earnings to support its growth.

Valuation Summary

I believe that Utz is fairly traded at the moment compared to its peers based on forward P/E.

Its premium P/E relative to its low-end peers reflects Utz’s growth potential.

The discount to high-end peers like VITL is coming from the fact that Utz is not going toward exceptional growth, with a modest forward revenue growth of 1.5%, which is slightly below the 1.7% sector median.

Additionally, Utz is positioned to invest heavily in its distribution network and capacity to support its expansion. Utz carries a high debt of around $1B. This is understandable in order to finance its growth, though it poses a risk of high-interest expense that could hinder its forward EPS growth.

In conclusion, I’m rating Utz a Hold.

The bullish case is to see better net margin, accompanied by high revenue growth above the sector's median that exceeds analysts’ expectations.

The bearish case involves slow or no revenue growth, which would signal a failed strategy to gain additional market share.

data.title: Hammond Power Solutions: High Voltage Fundamentals, Low Voltage Multiple
data.description:
Introduction

Hammond Power Solutions (TSX: TSX:HPS.A:CA / OTCPK: OTCPK:HMDPF) isn’t going to show up in anyone’s “next AI winner” screen. They make dry-type transformers and power-quality gear — old-school electrical hardware that keeps modern systems from frying. It’s not glamorous, but it’s hard to electrify anything at scale without this kind of equipment quietly working in the background. And right now, there’s more demand than supply across the board — EV charging, data centers, grid upgrades, microgrids — pick your secular trend.

Despite that setup, the stock still trades at ~15x earnings and under 10x EBITDA, with a clean balance sheet, record backlog, and new capacity just starting to come online. Execution has been consistent, earnings are climbing, and the margin profile is better than most peers — many of which trade at materially higher multiples. There are some working capital swings and India’s soft, but nothing thesis-breaking. At this valuation, with FY2026 earnings likely above $8/share, the upside looks good. I’m rating HPS a Buy.

Business Operations and Market Conditions

Hammond Power is the largest dry-type transformer maker in North America. These are air-cooled units — no oil — which makes them ideal for indoor or sensitive environments like server farms, battery storage, EV fast-charging, and renewables. Over half of what they ship is custom, and the scale isn’t trivial: more than 450,000 transformers and 9,000 SKUs go out the door each year.

The company also sells cast resin variants and a line of power quality components — mostly filters, reactors, and voltage suppression systems that help stabilize industrial equipment. That part of the business took a step back in 2024 thanks to project delays, but the product line was expanded, and production was shifted down to the new Mexico plant to support better margins when volumes recover.

By geography, about 68% of sales come from the U.S. and Mexico, 27% from Canada, and 5% from India. Canada grew the fastest in 2024, but India was down due to timing issues and election-related slowdowns. They’re not relying on India for growth at this point, but longer-term it probably matters.

Customers break out into three channels — distributors (69%), OEMs (20%), and private label (11%). That’s a nice spread in theory, though in practice, distributor and project business can blur when products are semi-custom and tied to specific timelines. Custom orders held up well even as standard stock softened late in 2024. Backlog jumped 17.7% sequentially in Q1 2025, which is probably the most telling data point in the whole setup.

To meet that demand, Hammond’s investing around C$80 million over three years. The Monterrey plant is the centerpiece — small transformer and power-quality capacity aimed mostly at the U.S. — and they’ve added warehouse space in Dallas. Lead times should come down, freight costs drop, and fulfillment gets easier. Assuming it works.

They’re not a startup, but they’re not a global giant either. With ~23% share of the North American dry-type market, Hammond sits between regional independents and the multinational behemoths like ABB and Siemens. They win with customization, speed, and relationships — not price. It’s not a wide moat, but it’s defensible.

Financials

FY2024 was solid. Revenue grew 11.0% to C$788.3 million, gross margins ticked up to 32.8%, and earnings from operations rose almost 14% to C$98.8 million. Net income came in at C$71.5 million (C$6.01/share), up 12.8%. Adjusted EBITDA was C$130.5 million — 16.5% margin. Nothing in there is a surprise, but it’s clean and directionally strong.

Metric FY2024 FY2023 YoY Change
Revenue C$788.3 million C$710.1 million +11.0%
Gross Profit C$258.6 million C$230.8 million +12.0%
Gross Margin 32.8% 32.5% +30 bps
Adjusted EBITDA C$130.5 million C$117.2 million +11.3%
Net Income C$71.5 million C$63.4 million +12.8%
Backlog (FYE) C$174.7 million C$174.9 million -0.1%

Q1 2025 held the line. Revenue was up 5.6% YoY to C$201.4 million, helped by U.S. (+7.8%) and Canada (+6.9%) — more than offsetting a steep 23.9% drop in India. Net income was C$26.2 million (C$2.20/share), and adjusted EBITDA was C$30.9 million — 15.4% margin, down a bit but still fine. Gross margin held at 31.5%. Some mix pressure and heavier opex, but nothing alarming.

Metric Q1 2025 Q1 2024 YoY Change
Revenue C$201.4 million C$190.7 million +5.6%
Gross Profit C$63.5 million C$60.4 million +5.1%
Gross Margin 31.5% 31.7% -20 bps
Adjusted EBITDA C$30.9 million C$31.0 million -0.3%
Net Income C$26.2 million C$8.0 million +227.5%
Backlog (Q1-end) C$205.8 million C$173.2 million +18.8%

Backlog at the end of Q1 hit C$205.8 million — up 17.7% sequentially and 18.8% YoY. That’s the highest level they’ve ever reported, and it’s not fluff. Most of it’s tied to utility, infrastructure, and data center demand — all long-cycle, which helps buffer the weaker industrial orders.

Cash Flow and Capex

FY2024 cash flow looked good — C$64.8 million from operations, which easily covered C$40.6 million in capex and C$11.6 million in dividends. The balance sheet stayed clean.

Q1 2025 was more complicated. Operating cash flow swung to negative C$(3.0) million, mostly due to a C$30.4 million working capital draw. Capex was C$7.9 million. So total free cash outflow was C$(10.9) million. Not great, but also not a surprise given where they are in the expansion cycle. Inventories were built up for long-lead projects, and receivables jumped — probably timing-related, but worth watching.

They drew down more on their credit line — C$28 million outstanding versus C$13 million at year-end — but still held C$32.8 million in cash. That leaves them with ~C$4.8 million net cash and over C$100 million in unused availability. So they’re fine on liquidity, just not in accumulation mode right now.

Metric Q1 2025 Q1 2024 YoY Change
Cash and Cash Equivalents C$32.8 million C$34.1 million -3.8%
Bank Operating Lines Drawn C$28.0 million C$13.0 million +115.4%
Net Cash (ex-leases) C$4.8 million C$21.1 million -77.3%
Inventory C$155.4 million C$137.6 million +12.9%
Accounts Receivable C$147.8 million C$138.1 million +7.0%

Management expects working capital to normalize once Monterrey ramps and fulfillment starts flowing through Dallas. If they get back to a C$25–30 million free cash flow run-rate by FY2026, none of this matters much. But that’s still an “if” until we see how the execution plays out.

Valuation

HPS trades at 15.05x trailing P/E, 9.87x EV/EBITDA, and 1.69x sales — all modest, especially for a company with no debt and 11% revenue growth. Industrial comps trade higher across the board — median sector P/E is 23.35x, EV/EBITDA at 13.1x. Even on forward estimates, Hammond screens attractively.

Powell Industries (POWL) 2.03 12.71 8.35 44.8% 174.8%
Indo Tech Transformers (INDOTECH.NS) 3.53 33.80 22.34 35.8% 82.3%

Voltamp Transformers (VOLTAMP.NS) 4.55 27.03 19.12 19.7% 5.9%

TRANS & RECTI (TARIL.NS) 68.11 68.11 42.74 56% 360.4%

Peer comps vary. Powell Industries is cheaper on EV/EBITDA (8.35x), but their business is more lumpy — bigger projects, broader scope. Indian peers like Voltamp, Shilchar, and Indo Tech all trade at much higher multiples — 19–30x EBITDA — which reflects both higher returns and a more speculative domestic market. Even Bharat Bijlee and Pioneer Power trade above Hammond on EV/EBITDA, despite arguably lower-quality earnings.

Hammond’s top-line growth (+11%) and net income growth (+12.8%) are better than most U.S. or Canadian industrials. And yet, valuation still lags — probably because of size, liquidity, Canadian listing, and perceived cyclicality. That said, the business is less tied to public utility capex cycles than some larger players, and it’s basically unlevered.

With Q1 already showing C$2.20 in EPS, a full-year figure closer to C$8.00 seems reasonable. At 14x, that’s a C$112 stock. Right now, it trades well below that. If margins expand with volume, a move to 18–20x earnings or 11–12x EBITDA wouldn’t be crazy. But the market probably waits for more proof before rerating.

Catalysts

A few things could help move the stock higher in the coming year. First, the Monterrey factory will be critical. Once it begins shipping power-quality filters and smaller transformers in late 2025, the plant should improve both delivery times and margins, particularly for U.S. orders. Domestic production also aligns well with U.S. nearshoring and Buy America trends.

Second, the product mix continues to shift in the right direction. Mesta filter lines are expanding, new frame sizes have been introduced, and demand from EV charging infrastructure and data centers remains strong. These higher-value segments should boost average selling prices and margin contribution over time.

Third, operating leverage is starting to show. FY2024 saw gross margin improvement despite flat power-quality sales and input inflation. If volume continues to grow and overhead is spread across more output, there’s room for additional gains — especially as the new capacity ramps up.

Finally, the 17.7% sequential backlog increase in Q1 signals strong demand. Custom project backlogs, which stretch across multiple quarters, help provide visibility and reduce reliance on short-cycle demand. That kind of backlog is rare in a micro-cap industrial, and could smooth revenue and earnings into FY2026.

Risks

Still, there are things that could go wrong. End-market demand is cyclical — construction, industrial capex, utilities, mining. If one or more slows down meaningfully, that would show up in order intake. Standard transformer sales already softened in late 2024.

Input costs are another issue. Copper, aluminum, and electrical steel remain volatile. HPS has done a good job passing along price increases, but there’s always a lag, and margin pressure is a risk in tighter environments.

The Monterrey plant is a swing factor. If it runs well, great. If there are delays, cost overruns, or integration issues, that could hurt both earnings and perception. Same goes for Mesta. Power-quality is a growth lever, but 2024 was a down year and it hasn’t rebounded yet.

India’s a weak spot. Q1 sales were down 23.9%, and while some of that’s elections and project delays, it’s not clear when or how that business regains momentum. Not critical yet, but disappointing.

Receivables have also been growing faster than revenue. Up 7% sequentially and 20% YoY. Could just be project billing timing, but worth keeping an eye on — especially if more revenue is tied to large custom systems.

Conclusion

Hammond Power’s not flashy, but it is delivering. Backlog’s at a high, Q1 came in clean, and the company’s doing what it said it would — building capacity, holding margins, and staying out of trouble. There’s no long-term debt, they’re funding everything internally, and gross margins are near cycle highs even with mix headwinds.

If Monterrey ramps as planned and power-quality recovers, earnings should move higher, and so should the multiple. At 18–20x forward earnings — which is hardly aggressive for a capital-light electrical name — the stock would be worth somewhere in the C$110–120 range. That’s ~40–50% upside without needing heroics. I rate HPS a Buy.
